heading: MODULO OTTAVO
card_summary: This chunk of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi treats the "guests" (hospites) of the heavenly city in four concentus: the incalculable number of angelic and saintly inhabitants (drawing on Revelation, Daniel, Jerome, and Pseudo-Dionysius); the angelic hierarchy of nine orders, the seven planetary governors drawn from Hermes Trismegistus, and the function of named archangels Gabriel, Raphael, and Michael; the graduated company of human residents — patriarchs, prophets, apostles, evangelists, martyrs, spouses, and virgins — arranged in harmonious mutual correspondence; and the Trinity as the supreme divine guests from whom all praise and being flow and to whom all return. Zorzi weaves together Revelation, Daniel, the Book of Esther (allegorized as Church history), Simeon ben Yochay on demons, the Hermetic Poimandres on the seven demiurgic governors, Pseudo-Dionysius, and the Hebrew word ruach to build a vision of universal cosmic harmony whose musical consummation is the "diapason perfetto" of all creatures returning to their source.
essay:
## Overview

Modulo Ottavo of the Cantico Terzo develops the theme of the "ospiti" — the inhabitants or guests — of the heavenly city. Structured as four *concentus*, the section moves from the unknowable quantity of the blessed, through the angelology and its hierarchies, to the ordered ranks of the human saints, and finally to the Trinity as the divine source and goal of all celestial harmony. The organizing metaphor is musical: each class of guest contributes to a cosmic choir, and the whole resolves in the "diapason perfetto" — the perfect octave — formed by all creatures' return to their origin.

---

## Concentus I: The Incalculable Number of Guests (pp. 2579–2581)

Zorzi opens by citing Revelation 7:9 ("una folla immensa che nessuno poteva contare") and God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 15:5) to establish that the population of the heavenly city is beyond human reckoning. He dismisses arithmetical schemes that purport to calculate the number of angels — one tradition bases each angelic legion on 6,666 spirits, gives each of nine choirs a squared legion, yielding a total of 399,920,004; another reads Daniel 7:10 ("mille migliaia lo servivano e dieci volte centomila gli stavano davanti") as yielding exactly two million. Zorzi ridicules the latter as arithmetically confused and invokes Jerome's sober comment:

> Non ex hoc Danielis dicto numerus ministrorum Dei diffinitus est, sed maiorem multitudinem humanus sermo explicare nequivit.

<cite>p. 2412 (Latin apparatus)</cite>

The true number, he concludes, is known only to God — the one who counts raindrops and grains of dust — and is reinforced by Psalm 68:18 ("I carri di Dio sono miriadi e migliaia di migliaia"). The guests are declared to be of three kinds: divine, angelic, and human.

---

## Concentus II: The Angelic Guests (pp. 2581–2586)

### The Seven Planetary Governors

Angelic minds are first divided into *assistentes* (those standing before God) and *ministrantes* (those sent on missions). Foremost among the assistants are the seven spirits linked to the seven planetary spheres, identified with the "seven lamps before the throne" of Revelation 4:5 and the "seven eyes and seven horns of the Lamb" of Revelation 5:6. Their governance unifies the Trinitarian ternary and the elemental quaternary into the number seven.

Zorzi then adduces Hermes Trismegistus — identified here as "Mercurio" — from the *Poimandres* to authenticate the seven demiurgic governors:

> Mens (inquit) Deus utriusque sexus foecunditate plenissimus, vita, et lux cum verbo suo mentem alteram opificem fabricavit, qui quidem Deus, ignis, atque spiritus numen est. Septem deinceps fabricavit gubernatores, qui circulis mundum sensibilem complectuntur, quorum dispositio fatum vocatur.

<cite>p. 2413 (Latin apparatus)</cite>

This Hermetic "other demiurgic mind" is identified with the Holy Spirit — an audacious equation that allows Zorzi to read Pagan cosmology as a shadow of Trinitarian theology. The Wisdom of Solomon's description of the Spirit as "omnium artifex" reinforces this reading.

### Allegorical Typology: Esther, the Seven Churches, the Seven Deacons

Zorzi then pivots to Scripture typology. The Book of Esther — "ricolmo di misteri" — presents the seven ministers of King Ahasuerus as figures of the seven governing angels; Vasti (the repudiated queen) typifies the Synagogue and Esther the Church. The seven churches of Revelation (governed by seven angels) and the seven deacons elected in Acts correspond symmetrically to this heavenly governance structure. Named archangels appear: Gabriel is sent to Zechariah, Daniel, Joseph, and above all Mary; Raphael to Tobias; Michael to assist Gabriel (Daniel 10:13). Their occasional missions do not contradict their status as courtiers, just as cardinals dispatched by the pope retain their standing in the curia.

### The Nine Orders of Angels

The nine Dionysian orders are then enumerated:

> Primo sono i serafini, che ricevono dal fuoco divino l'ardore del quale essi stessi bruciano e ci infiammano. Vi sono poi i cherubini, che ricevono le forme e le idee mediante le quali il loro intelletto variegato delinea e decora anche il nostro. Vi si trovano poi i troni...

<cite>p. 2417 (Italian text)</cite>

Seraphim draw from the divine fire; Cherubim receive forms and ideas; Thrones provide stable seats for God and dispose humanity to become his temple. Of the remaining six orders (Dominions, Virtues, Powers, Principalities, Archangels, Angels), Dominions act as architects directing others; Virtues move the heavens and transmit celestial influences; Powers repel disorder; Principalities govern nations, provinces, and kingdoms; Archangels preside over rulers and sacred rites; Angels serve as individual guardian spirits.

Zorzi also mentions 72 angelic princes (a standard Kabbalistic figure), angels given power to harm earth and sea (Revelation 7:2), and angels who "somministrano ai più esili fili d'erba, ai sassolini e a tutte le realtà inferiori il loro potere" — a Neoplatonic angelology extending down to the lowest natural phenomena.

### Demons as Counterpart

The section closes with a symmetrical demonic population: for every pure spirit above there is an impure spirit below, filling the "cratere inferiore e impuro." Simeon ben Yochay (*Simeon Beniochai*), identified as the supreme Hebrew theologian, is cited for the claim that 200,000 unclean spirits were revealed to him in a column of smoke. The Legion of Gerasene swine (Mark 5) clinches the argument about the uncountable multitude of demons.

---

## Concentus III: The Human Guests (pp. 2587–2591)

The blessed humans are arranged in harmonious ranks that make earthly kingdoms mere "pallida ombra" by comparison. The hierarchy runs: Christ the King of Kings; Mary (daughter, spouse, and mother of the eternal King); Patriarchs as "padri dei padri"; Prophets and Apostles as the paired foundations (*supra fundamenta apostolorum et prophetarum*, Ephesians 2:20) united by the bond that the apostles explicated what the prophets had foretold in obscurity; Evangelists as royal archivists (Zorzi notes Matthew was an apostle, Luke a disciple); Priests and Kings of both Testaments, "unti e cristi del Signore"; Martyrs; Spouses (whose matrimonial sacrament figured the celestial marriage of Christ and Church); Widows; and finally the 144,000 virgins who alone can sing the new song before the throne (Revelation 14:3–4):

> Vi sono poi anche i vergini, cioè quanti non hanno contaminato se stessi con le donne, i quali cantano quel cantico nuovo, che nessuno poteva cantare se non quei centoquarantaquattromila che seguono l'Agnello ovunque vada e sono senza macchia davanti al trono di Dio.

<cite>p. 2421 (Italian text)</cite>

The doxology of Revelation 7:12 — "Amen! La benedizione, la gloria, la sapienza..." — frames the whole, and Zorzi notes the numerological significance of *amen* as containing within its letter-values the divine names of true dominion.

---

## Concentus IV: The Divine Guests (pp. 2591–2592)

The final movement identifies the Trinity itself as the supreme "divine guests" from whom all praise, being, and life flow, and toward whom all return. The Father is the source of existence for those who praise; the Son provides the measures, art, and rules of harmonious singing; the Holy Spirit supplies the breath (*flatum*) that gives voice. The Hebrew etymology is invoked:

> Spiritus, infatti, nella lingua che è detta santa ed è propria delle sacre Scritture, si dice ruach, che significa 'vento' e 'soffio'.

<cite>p. 2422 (Italian text)</cite>

The Spirit's transformative action is illustrated by a typological chain: the boy David (citharist → psalmist), Moses (shepherd → lawgiver), Amos (herdsman pruning sycamores → prophet), the fishermen-apostles, Paul (persecutor → teacher of nations), Matthew (tax collector → evangelist). The source is the same Spirit "che distribuisce ai singoli e opera tutto in tutti" (1 Corinthians 12:11).

The section concludes with Pseudo-Dionysius: "Tutti godono per impulso del Padre." All the blessed receive everything from the Trisagion (the threefold holy of Isaiah 6 / Revelation 4:8) and all return to it "come altrettante linee al centro, dal quale emanano." This convergence of emanation and return — the Neoplatonic *processio* and *regressio* — produces the organizing musical resolution of the whole section:

> Il ritorno al medesimo punto dal quale presero le mosse rende il diapason perfetto.

<cite>p. 2423 (Italian text)</cite>

---

## Key Sources and Technical Terms

- **Scripture**: Revelation 4:5, 5:6, 7:9, 7:12, 14:3–4; Daniel 7:10; Genesis 15:5; Ephesians 2:20; Galatians 4:22–23; Psalm 68:18; Acts 6; Matthew 10:20; 1 Corinthians 12:6, 12:11; Wisdom 7:22, 7:27
- **Hermes Trismegistus** (*Poimandres*): seven demiurgic governors; identification of "other demiurgic mind" with Holy Spirit
- **Pseudo-Dionysius Areopagita**: nine angelic orders; the incalculability of the heavenly host; "Gaudent omnes movente Patre"
- **Jerome**: on Daniel 7:10 and the indefiniteness of angelic number
- **Simeon ben Yochay** (R. Shimon bar Yochai): the 200,000 demons in a column of smoke — cited as "il più grande teologo degli ebrei"
- **Hebrew**: *ruach* (רוח) — wind, breath, spirit; the numerological value of *amen* (אמן)
- **Greek/Latin musical term**: *diapason* — the octave, here the harmonic resolution of all creatures' return to the Trinity
- **Kabbalistic**: 72 angelic princes; Simeon ben Yochay's demonology
- **Typology**: Esther/Vasti as Church/Synagogue; seven ministers of Ahasuerus as seven planetary angels; seven deacons of Acts as earthly reflex of heavenly governance

The chunk is a microcosm of Zorzi's entire project: the celestial harmony is simultaneously scriptural, Hermetic, Kabbalistic, Neoplatonic, and ecclesiastical, and the musical metaphor of the *diapason* draws all these registers into a single cosmological vision.
